# R2.03 · The Redlock algorithm (contrast)

> A fault-tolerant distributed lock that acquires on a majority (N/2+1) of N independent Redis masters, tolerating a
> minority of node failures. Taught as a CONTRAST: EchoMQ does not implement Redlock — its lock is a single-Valkey
> claim lease (`@claim`'s `ZADD active now+lease_ms` on one server clock, fenced by `attempts`), and a job queue makes
> its work idempotent so a rare lost lease is a recovered double-run, not corruption. Served at
> /redis-patterns/coordination/redlock.

## Pages

- [Hub](/redis-patterns/coordination/redlock): the majority-of-N lock, its timing assumptions, and why a single-Valkey lease is enough for a queue.
- [Majority of N](/redis-patterns/coordination/redlock/majority-of-n): acquire on N/2+1 of N independent masters; the T1/acquire/T2 elapsed-time guard; release on all instances on failure.
- [Clock assumptions](/redis-patterns/coordination/redlock/clock-assumptions): bounded clock drift, the validity math (TTL − elapsed − drift), and the Kleppmann–Sanfilippo debate — which EchoMQ sidesteps by reading ONE server clock (`redis.call('TIME')`) inside one atomic script.
- [Single instance enough](/redis-patterns/coordination/redlock/single-instance-enough): when the single-Valkey lease is sufficient and Redlock's five-instance cost is not justified — EchoMQ's deliberate choice (the claim lease + `attempts` fence).

## Up

- [R2 · Coordination](/redis-patterns/coordination) · [Course home](/redis-patterns)
